source.title: Fatal Equipment Rupture, Explosion, and Fire at the KMCO Chemical Facility

event.top_event: Equipment rupture and flammable vapor cloud explosion
actions_taken: Manual closure of isolation valve to stop isobutylene release
Vehicle gates closed to prevent entry into vapor cloud
Equipment shutdown and evacuation instructions issued
Medical assistance provided to injured workers
event.summary: On April 2, 2019, at 10:51 a.m., a 3-inch cast iron y-strainer in the isobutylene supply piping ruptured due to brittle overload fracture driven by internal pressure from liquid thermal expansion. The rupture released more than 10,000 pounds of flammable isobutylene, creating a vapor cloud. KMCO operators manually closed valves and activated firewater systems to mitigate the release. However, the isobutylene vapor cloud ignited while two operators were still in or near the vapor cloud, fatally injuring one operator (James Mangum) and seriously injuring two others. At least 28 workers total were injured. The explosion and subsequent fires caused substantial facility damage. KMCO filed for bankruptcy in May 2020.
recommendations: Establish clear emergency response policies distinguishing which incidents operators should handle versus those requiring emergency response team response
Provide remote isolation equipment (remotely operated valves) to allow workers to stop chemical releases from safe locations such as blast-resistant control rooms
Implement comprehensive hazard evaluation programs that identify brittle materials in hazardous applications and potential high-pressure conditions
Install pressure-relief devices on piping sections subject to potential high-pressure conditions from thermal expansion
Ensure emergency response procedures and training prioritize life safety and do not rely on operators putting themselves in danger to stop releases
Activate facility alarm systems during chemical emergencies to ensure all workers receive notification

hazards.name: Brittle cast iron equipment in hazardous service

threats.name: Liquid thermal expansion creating high pressure
Inadequate material selection for hazardous service
Electrical equipment ignition of vapor cloud
Operator exposure to vapor cloud during emergency response

consequences.name: Serious burn injuries to two operators
Substantial facility damage from explosion and fire
Community impact - shelter-in-place order for 1-mile radius

controls.name: Cast iron y-strainer in isobutylene inlet piping
controls.barrier_role: Filtration device to protect charge pump

supporting_text: a fist-sized piece of metal broke away from the body of a 3-inch cast iron y-strainer
The cause of the isobutylene release was a brittle overload fracture of the cast iron y-strainer driven by internal pressure
cast iron, a brittle material that existing industry standards and good practice guidance documents either prohibit or warn against using in hazardous applications

controls.name: Pressure-relief valve on charge pump outlet

supporting_text: KMCO installed a pressure-relief valve on the charge pump's outlet piping
this piping section was not equipped with a pressure-relief device or otherwise protected from potential high-pressure conditions

controls.name: Pressure-relief protection on isobutylene piping section containing y-strainer
controls.barrier_role: Protect y-strainer piping from high-pressure conditions

supporting_text: unlike other portions of KMCO's isobutylene piping, this piping section was not equipped with a pressure-relief device or otherwise protected from potential high-pressure conditions

none of KMCO's hazard evaluations identified the potential for liquid thermal expansion or other possible scenarios to develop high-pressure conditions within the piping system that included the y-strainer

controls.name: Remote isolation valve (actuated block valve) upstream of y-strainer
controls.barrier_role: Allow operators to stop isobutylene release from safe location

supporting_text: had KMCO workers been able to close the actuated block valve installed just upstream of the y-strainer from the control room, the amount of isobutylene released and the subsequent harm to workers could have been greatly reduced
KMCO's workers lacked the safety equipment they needed to stop (isolate) the isobutylene release from a safe location, such as from within the blast-resistant control room

KMCO had an existing recommendation from a 2010 report developed for insurance underwriting purposes that called for providing remote isolation equipment for certain flammable liquid systems, including isobutylene. Although KMCO had installed remote isolation equipment for some of these systems, the company had not yet addressed this recommendation for its isobutylene system

controls.name: Manual isolation valve closure by field operators
controls.barrier_role: Stop isobutylene release by manual valve closure

supporting_text: Board Operator 1 closed a manual isolation valve in the unit
Operations team members entered the release area and manually closed valves. This stopped the flow of isobutylene
Board Operator 1 had also manually closed valves to stop the isobutylene release

controls.name: Firewater deluge system activation
controls.barrier_role: Suppress vapor cloud and cool equipment

supporting_text: The operations team members also opened other valves, allowing firewater to spray throughout the batch reactor area
Field Operator 1 then teamed up with a senior field operator to turn on firewater monitors
Board Operator 1 and Board Operator 2 were turning firewater monitors on and activating water deluge systems in their respective units
Board Operator 1 identified the leak as isobutylene and, using the all-call feature on Field Operator 1's radio, announced: 'Attention KMCO, evacuate the reaction area'
the Shift Supervisor used the all-call feature on his radio to order an evacuation of the plant at about 10:47 a.m.
not all workers at the site carried a radio or were with someone carrying a radio. As a result, some workers did not receive the emergency notification radio message to evacuate from the plant

controls.name: Plant alarm system for emergency notification
controls.barrier_role: Notify all workers of emergency situation

supporting_text: KMCO had a plant alarm system that, among other purposes, was supposed to ensure all workers could be notified of emergency situations. But KMCO never activated its plant alarm system during the isobutylene release, explosion, or subsequent fires. Instead, KMCO relied on its plant radio system to make emergency notifications

KMCO's hazard evaluations consistently overlooked or misunderstood that its y-strainer was made from cast iron, a brittle material that existing industry standards and good practice guidance documents either prohibit or warn against using in hazardous applications

none of KMCO's hazard evaluations identified the potential for liquid thermal expansion or other possible scenarios to develop high-pressure conditions within the piping system that included the y-strainer
KMCO's hazard evaluations consistently overlooked or misunderstood that its y-strainer was made from cast iron

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC) process
controls.barrier_role: Evaluate safety implications of changes to process

supporting_text: KMCO's Process Safety Management program... also contributed to the incident
Isobutylene System MOC and PSSR
KMCO's hazard evaluation programs, including process hazard analysis, pre-startup safety review, and management of change, also contributed to the incident

controls.name: Emergency response procedures and training
controls.barrier_role: Guide appropriate response to chemical releases

supporting_text: KMCO's emergency response procedures and training did not properly limit the role of its operators during the emergency response
KMCO's plant culture relied on unit operators taking quick actions to stop a release before the site's emergency response team assembled

Deficiencies in KMCO's emergency response system, including its procedures and training, also contributed to the severity of the incident by not effectively distinguishing which events its operators should handle from those incidents that the site's emergency response team must respond to

controls.name: Blast-resistant control room
controls.barrier_role: Provide safe location for operators during emergency

supporting_text: Following a facility siting study, KMCO built a blast-resistant control room in 2013 to improve safety

had KMCO workers been able to close the actuated block valve installed just upstream of the y-strainer from the control room, the amount of isobutylene released and the subsequent harm to workers could have been greatly reduced

controls.name: Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA) availability
controls.barrier_role: Provide respiratory protection during emergency response

supporting_text: Board Operator 1 put an SCBA on for respiratory protection
A self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) was also located in this building

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
